title: Nail Polish on Cashmere
timeEstimate: Pro IMMEDIATELY — do not improvise at home
stainChemistry: Nail polish is nitrocellulose + plasticizers + pigment + ester solvents (sometimes acetone). Cashmere is the most delicate keratin protein fiber — finer than wool (14–15 microns vs 20–30), felts on lighter friction, more solvent-sensitive than wool. Acetone bleeds cashmere dyes AND damages keratin. "Non-acetone" remover has uncontrolled ester concentrations that strip cashmere color. IPA bleeds cashmere dyes. Water flooding = felt risk on contact. The home toolkit for nail polish on any surface DESTROYS cashmere. Pro uses cold amyl acetate at strict dilution + tight blotting + cold NSD — chemistry that's cashmere-tuned and home cannot do safely.
whyThisWorks: A cashmere specialist with cold amyl acetate + minimum-moisture blotting + cold NSD can lift fresh polish without felting or stripping cashmere dye. Home protocol = lift bulk if wet, leave alone if dry, pro IMMEDIATELY. For any meaningful cashmere garment, applying NO chemistry at home gives the pro the best shot.
defaultAssumption: Cashmere + nail polish = pro IMMEDIATELY. Home applies NO solvent. Best outcome reaches the cleaner with nothing applied.

spottingProtocol.technique: Amyl acetate hidden test — extra strict on cashmere
spottingProtocol.equipment: Amyl acetate, white cloth, hidden seam
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply amyl acetate to a hidden seam for 30 sec. Check for color bleed, fiber change, or texture shift. Cashmere is more solvent-sensitive than wool — abort on any sign.

spottingProtocol.technique: Tight blotting, no flooding, minimum solvent
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 5–10 minutes total, refresh cloth each pass
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply to film with white cloth, tight blotting concentric passes. Refresh cloth. Do NOT flood cashmere — water/solvent + agitation = felt.
Sponge gently with minimum moisture. Press only — never rub cashmere.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Acetic Acid (diluted 1:20 — MORE dilute than wool)
spottingProtocol.instruction: More dilute than wool (1:20). Dropper for pH restoration. STRICT 60-sec dwell. Press dry, lay flat, no heat.

steps: 1. Hidden-seam amyl acetate test — abort on any sign of bleed / felt / texture change.
2. Amyl acetate tight-tamp blotting, no flooding.
3. Cold NSD sponge with minimum moisture.
5. Press dry, lay flat to air-dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient solvent + cold rinse — no heat
warnings: NEVER acetone on cashmere — bleeds dye + damages keratin (more sensitive than wool)
NEVER nail polish remover at home — uncontrolled esters
NEVER isopropyl alcohol on cashmere — bleeds dyes
NEVER hot water — felts cashmere in seconds
NEVER agitation — felts cashmere on lighter friction than wool
Hidden-seam test mandatory and stricter than wool

homeSolutions.instruction: Cashmere + nail polish is the worst home stain pairing in this entire library. Cashmere is the most delicate fiber + nail polish is the most chemistry-resistant common stain. Every household solvent damages cashmere: acetone bleeds dye + damages keratin, "non-acetone" remover strips color, isopropyl alcohol bleeds dye, water flooding felts the fiber. The cotton + silk + wool playbooks all damage cashmere worse than the polish does. Best move: leave the stain ALONE, take to a cashmere specialist within 24 hours.

homeSolutions.agent: If Wet — Press Once Gently, Then Stop
homeSolutions.instruction: If the polish is still liquid, press a dry white cloth STRAIGHT DOWN on the pool, outside-in. Lift straight up — no wiping, no smearing. 1–2 light dabs. Do NOT add water, do NOT rub, do NOT press deep. Surface bulk only. Even gentle pressure on damp cashmere can felt the fiber.

homeSolutions.agent: If Dry — Leave It Completely Alone
homeSolutions.instruction: Dried polish on cashmere is EASIER for a pro to handle than disturbed polish. Do NOT pick at it with a fingernail. Do NOT scrape — scraping shreds cashmere fibers (finer than wool, even more vulnerable). Do NOT attempt to soften with anything.

Cashmere specialists are rare. Many cleaners decline cashmere entirely. Call ahead: "Do you treat cashmere with amyl acetate for nail polish?" If they don't understand, try another cleaner. When you bring it in, tell them: "Nail polish on cashmere, [wet still / dried]. NOTHING applied at home. No acetone, no remover, no alcohol, no water, no scrubbing."

steps: 1. Cashmere + nail polish = pro IMMEDIATELY. NO home solvents.
2. If wet: 1–2 LIGHT dabs straight down with white cloth. Stop.
3. If dry: leave alone. Do not pick, scrape, or soften.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Always stop after the initial blot. Any home solvent on cashmere creates new damage worse than the polish.
warnings: NEVER acetone on cashmere — bleeds dye + damages keratin
NEVER nail polish remover (any kind) on cashmere
NEVER alcohol / IPA on cashmere — bleeds dyes
NEVER hot water — felts cashmere in seconds
Hidden-seam test belongs to the pro

neverDo: Never nail polish remover at home
fiberSensitivities: Cashmere keratin fibers are 14–15 microns — felt faster than wool (20–30)
Cashmere dye bleeds at lighter solvent exposure than wool

materialWarnings: Pro IMMEDIATELY — no safe home solvent
Cotton + silk + wool playbooks all damage cashmere
Don't pick or scrape dried polish — shreds cashmere
Don't flood cashmere with water — felt risk

escalation.when: Always — cashmere + nail polish is pro-only IMMEDIATELY. The cleaner's best outcome is the one where you bring it in with NOTHING applied.
escalation.whatToTell: "Nail polish on cashmere, [wet still / dried], age [hours / days]. NOTHING applied at home. No acetone, no remover, no alcohol, no water, no scrubbing."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in cashmere (call ahead — many decline cashmere; confirm amyl acetate spotting + nitrocellulose-on-cashmere experience)

contextQuestions: Cashmere weight — fine knit, heavier knit, blend?
Has anyone tried anything yet (acetone, alcohol, remover)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Pre-applied acetone or remover = cashmere dye likely already bled where applied. Pre-applied IPA = cashmere likely partially dye-stripped. Pre-applied water + rubbing = cashmere may already be partially felted. Disclose honestly — dramatically changes pro plan. Fine-gauge knits felt faster than heavier knits.
